1: Bišnat hāʿăśirît lĕṣidqiyyāhû melek-yĕhûdâ hî haššānâ šĕmoneh ʿeśrēh šānâ lĕNĕḇuḵadrĕʾṣṣar (bišnat hāʿăśirît lĕṣidqiyyāhû): the dual dating — 10th year of Zedekiah = 18th year of Nebuchadnezzar — is 588 BCE, a year before Jerusalem falls. The precise dating is significant: this is an acted prophecy of hope performed at the darkest moment. The specificity of dating grounds the sign-act in verifiable historical reality.
2: Wĕḥêl melek-bāḇel ṣārîm ʿal-yĕrûšālāyim (wĕḥêl melek-bāḇel ṣārîm ʿal-yĕrûšālāyim): the Babylonian siege (ṣārîm, Qal participle — ongoing action) is the immediate context. WîrmĕyāhÛ hānnāḇîʾ hāyâ kālûʾ baḥăṣar hammaTTārâ (kālûʾ baḥăṣar hammaṭṭārâ): Jeremiah is confined (kālûʾ, Qal passive — locked up) in the court of the guard (ḥăṣar hammaṭṭārâ), a semi-open area within the palace complex (Neh 3:25). The imprisonment details sharpen the paradox: a man physically confined performs a sign-act of land-purchase that proclaims freedom and restoration.
3: ʾĂšer kĕlāʾô ṣidqiyyāhû melek-yĕhûdâ lēʾmōr (ʾĂšer kĕlāʾô ṣidqiyyāhû): Zedekiah's accusation is reproduced: maddûaʿ ʾattâ nibbāʾ lēʾmōr ("why do you keep prophesying?") — the Hitpael of nābāʾ is iterative/habitual action. The content of the prophetic word that earned imprisonment — "YHWH is handing this city to the king of Babylon" — is precisely the word being enacted in reverse through the field purchase. Zedekiah opposes the word; Jeremiah enacts hope through the same word.
4: Wĕṣidqiyyāhû melek-yĕhûdâ lōʾ-yimmāLēṭ miyyad hakkaśdîm (lōʾ-yimmāLēṭ miyyad hakkaśdîm): the oracle against Zedekiah is stated plainly — no escape. Kî hinnātēn yinnātēn bĕyad melek bāḇel (kî hinnātēn yinnātēn): the doubled infinitive absolute makes the surrender absolutely certain: "he will surely be given." Ûpîw ʾim-pîw yĕdabbēr wĕʿênāyw ʾet-ʿênāyw tirʾênâ (ûpîw ʾim-pîw yĕdabbēr wĕʿênāyw ʾet-ʿênāyw tirʾênâ): "his mouth will speak with his mouth and his eyes will see his eyes" — a personal, face-to-face encounter with Nebuchadnezzar, fulfilling the public humiliation of exile (2 Kings 25:6-7).
5: Wĕḇāḇel yôliḵ ʾet-ṣidqiyyāhû wĕšām yihyeh ʿad-pŏqĕdî ʾōtô (ʿad-pŏqĕdî ʾōtô): "until I attend to him" — pāqad (to attend to, to visit) is YHWH's sovereign act of intervention, either punitive or restorative. The ambiguity is intentional: Zedekiah will remain in Babylon until YHWH's visitation, whose nature is left open. Kî tiṣṣĕḇû ʾet-hakkaśdîm lōʾ taṣlîḥû (kî tiṣṣĕḇû ʾet-hakkaśdîm lōʾ taṣlîḥû): "if you fight the Chaldeans, you will not succeed" — military resistance is futile because this is YHWH's judgment, not merely Babylonian aggression.
6: Wayyōʾmer Yirmĕyāhû hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr (hāyâ dĕḇar-YHWH ʾēlay lēʾmōr): the classic prophetic reception formula introduces the private divine communication that preceded the public sign-act. Jeremiah's narration in the first person (vv6-15) distinguishes this passage from the surrounding third-person biographical prose, lending the account the weight of personal eyewitness testimony.
7: Hānāmēʾl ben-Šallûm dōdĕḵā bāʾ ʾēlêḵā lēʾmōr qnēh-lĕḵā ʾet-śādĕhî ʾăšer bāʿănātôt kî lĕḵā mišpaṭ haggĕʾullâ liqnôt (kî lĕḵā mišpaṭ haggĕʾullâ liqnôt): the key legal term: gĕʾullâ (gĕʾullâ) — the right of the kinsman-redeemer. Under Leviticus 25:25, when a family member was forced to sell land due to poverty, the nearest kinsman (gōʾēl) had both the right and obligation to purchase it and keep it within the family. Jeremiah as Hanamel's nearest relative has the mišpaṭ haggĕʾullâ (legal right of redemption). The theological weight: YHWH enacts his own gōʾēl role in the restoration (Isa 41:14; 43:1; 44:6), and Jeremiah's act of purchase is the prophetic enactment of that divine redemptive purpose.
8: Wayyābōʾ ʾēlay Ḥănāmĕʾl ben-dōdî kiddĕḇar YHWH ʾel-ḥăṣar hammaṭṭārâ (kiddĕḇar YHWH): "as the word of YHWH had said" — the prior divine word's exact fulfillment in the cousin's arrival is the confirmation of the divine word's reliability. Wāʾēdaʿ kî dĕḇar-YHWH hûʾ (Wāʾēdaʿ kî dĕḇar-YHWH hûʾ): "I knew that this was the word of YHWH" — the yādaʿ (to know) is not mere recognition but the relational, experiential certitude that this purchase is YHWH's commanded act. Faith is enacted through a legal transaction in the face of imminent catastrophe.
9: Wāʾeqneh ʾet-haśśādeh mēʾēt Ḥănāmĕʾl ben-dōdî ʾăšer bāʿănātôt (Wāʾeqneh ʾet-haśśādeh): the actual purchase. Wāʾeškōl-lô ʾet-hakkesep šiḇʿâ šeqālîm waʿăśārâ hakkesep (šiḇʿâ šeqālîm waʿăśārâ hakkesep): seventeen shekels of silver. The specific price (wāʾeškōl-lô, I weighed out to him) is verifiable detail — this is not symbolic; it is a legal transaction conducted with precision. Anathoth is already captured by the Babylonians; the purchase has no immediate commercial value. Its entire value is eschatological: it enacts trust in YHWH's future.
10: Wāʾeḵtōḇ bassēper wāʾeḥtōm wāʾāʿēd ʿēdîm wāʾeškōl hakkesep bĕmōznāyim (Wāʾeḵtōḇ bassēper wāʾeḥtōm wāʾāʿēd ʿēdîm): four verbs of legal procedure — I wrote (kātaḇ), I sealed (ḥātam), I brought in witnesses (ʿûd Hiphil), I weighed (šāqal). Each verb represents a standard step in ancient Near Eastern property law. The Babylonian and Assyrian parallel purchase documents confirm that this description is technically accurate to the period. The prophetic act is simultaneously a genuine legal transaction and a theological statement.
11: Wāʾeqqaḥ ʾet-sēper hammiqqnâ ʾet-hehātûm hammîṣwâ wĕhaḥuqqîm wĕʾet-haggālûy (ʾet-hehātûm hammîṣwâ wĕhaḥuqqîm wĕʾet-haggālûy): the purchase document came in two copies — a sealed copy containing the full terms and conditions (hammiṣwâ wĕhaḥuqqîm) and an open copy. This matches the standard ANE practice of a duplicate deed with one sealed for long-term preservation and one open for immediate reference. The sealed copy was the legally binding original; the open copy the working document.
12: Wāʾettēn ʾet-sēper hammiqqnâ ʾel-Bārûḵ ben-Nēriyyâ ben-Maḥsēyâ (ʾel-Bārûḵ ben-Nēriyyâ ben-Maḥsēyâ): Baruch the scribe — Jeremiah's closest associate, the one who wrote the scroll in ch36 — receives the deed in the presence of Hanamel, the witnesses, and all the Judeans in the court of the guard. The public nature of the transaction before multiple witnesses invests it with maximum legal force. Baruch's role as custodian of the documents connects the sign-act to the prophetic literary legacy.
13: Wāʾăṣawweh ʾet-Bārûḵ lĕʿênêhem lēʾmōr (Wāʾăṣawweh ʾet-Bārûḵ lĕʿênêhem): the formal instruction (ṣāwâ) given to Baruch publicly ("before their eyes") — this is not a private arrangement but a public prophetic declaration enacted through legal form. The oral instructions give the legal transaction its interpretive framework.
14: Kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl qāḥ ʾet-hassĕpārîm hāʾēlleh ʾēt sēper hammiqqnâ hazzeh wĕʾēt hehātûm wĕʾēt sēper haggālûy hazzeh wĕnĕtattām biḵlî-ḥāreś lĕmaʿan yaʿamĕdû yāmîm rabbîm (biḵlî-ḥāreś lĕmaʿan yaʿamĕdû yāmîm rabbîm): the clay jar (kĕlî ḥāreś) for long-term preservation — the same technology that preserved the Dead Sea Scrolls for two millennia. The purpose: lĕmaʿan yaʿamĕdû yāmîm rabbîm ("that they may stand for many days"). The documents are not just legal evidence for an immediate transaction; they are a long-term prophetic charter meant to outlast the current catastrophe.
15: Kî ḵōh ʾāmar YHWH ṣĕḇāʾôt ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl ʿôd yiqqānû ḇāttîm ûśādôt ûḵĕrāmîm bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt (ʿôd yiqqānû ḇāttîm ûśādôt ûḵĕrāmîm bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt): "houses and fields and vineyards will yet be bought in this land." The word that interprets the sign-act: the purchase is not an individual eccentricity but a divinely-ordered prophetic enactment of the future. The three categories — houses (bāttîm), fields (śādôt), vineyards (ḵĕrāmîm) — represent the full range of productive property. The prophecy anticipates not just land ownership but a functioning agricultural economy.
16: Wāʾetpallēl ʾel-YHWH ʾaḥărê titî ʾet-sēper hammiqqnâ ʾel-Bārûḵ ben-Nēriyyâ lēʾmōr (Wāʾetpallēl ʾel-YHWH ʾaḥărê): the prayer follows the sign-act. The sequence is significant: Jeremiah first obeys the command to purchase (then prays), which is the pattern of faith-obedience preceding understanding. The Hitpael of pālal (to pray, intercede) is the same form used in 7:16 when YHWH forbade intercession — the prohibition has been lifted by the new reality of the sign-act of restoration.
17: ʾĂhāh ʾădōnāy YHWH (ʾĂhāh ʾădōnāy YHWH): the unique prayer opening — ʾăhāh (Ah!/Alas!) combined with ʾădōnāy YHWH (Lord GOD) expresses a kind of reverent astonishment. It appears also in 1:6 (Jeremiah's initial objection to his call), 4:10, and 14:13 — always as a cry from the depth of bewilderment before the paradox of divine action. Hinnēh ʾattâ ʿāśîtā ʾet-haššāmayim wĕʾet-hāʾāreṣ bĕḵōḥăḵā haggādôl ûbiḏrōʿăḵā haNNĕṭûyâ lōʾ-yippālēʾ mimmĕḵā kol-dāḇār (lōʾ-yippālēʾ mimmĕḵā kol-dāḇār): the creation formula grounds YHWH's power — heaven and earth made by the great power (kōaḥ gādôl) and outstretched arm (zĕrôaʿ nĕṭûyâ). The lōʾ-yippālēʾ mimmĕḵā kol-dāḇār ("nothing is too difficult/wonderful for you") is the Niphal of pālāʾ (to be wonderful, beyond normal human capacity) — what is pālāʾ for humans is not so for YHWH. The prayer's answer in v27 echoes this exactly.
18: ʿŌśeh ḥesed lāʾălāpîm ûmĕšallēm ʿăwōn ʾāḇôt ʾel-ḥêq bĕnêhem ʾaḥărêhem (ûmĕšallēm ʿăwōn ʾāḇôt ʾel-ḥêq bĕnêhem): the tension of ḥesed (steadfast love) extended to thousands (lāʾălāpîm, cf. Exod 20:6) alongside punishment (šillēm, Piel of šālam, to repay, make whole) of ancestral iniquity into the laps of descendants. This is the Sinai tension of Exod 34:6-7, cited in Jeremiah's prayer as the theological framework of the crisis — how can the God of ḥesed punish at all, and how can the God of justice extend mercy? The new covenant of ch31 resolves this tension: the forgiveness is complete and the ancestral debt is cancelled.
19: Gĕdōl hāʿēṣâ wĕrab hāʿălîliyyâ ʾăšer-ʿênêḵā pĕqūḥôt ʿal-kol-darkê bĕnê-ʾādām lātēt lĕʾîš kiddĕrāḵāyw ûḵipĕrî maʿălālay (ʾăšer-ʿênêḵā pĕqūḥôt ʿal-kol-darkê bĕnê-ʾādām): YHWH's eyes are open to all the ways of human beings — omniscience as moral oversight. The divine council theology of Job 1-2, Ps 139, Zech 4:10 (seven eyes that range through the whole earth) underlies this. Lātēt lĕʾîš kiddĕrāḵāyw ûḵipĕrî maʿălālay (lātēt lĕʾîš kiddĕrāḵāyw): retributive justice is not mechanical fate but YHWH's personal, morally attentive response to each person's deeds.
20: ʾĂšer-śamtā ʾōtôt ûmôpĕtîm bĕʾereṣ miṣrāyim wĕʿad-hayyôm hazzeh ûḇĕyiśrāʾēl ûḇāʾādām (ʾōtôt ûmôpĕtîm bĕʾereṣ miṣrāyim): the exodus signs and wonders (ʾōtôt ûmôpĕtîm) — the Deuteronomic language of the ten plagues and the sea-crossing — are cited as evidence of YHWH's historical fidelity. Wattaʿăśeh-lĕḵā šēm kĕhayyôm hazzeh (Wattaʿăśeh-lĕḵā šēm kĕhayyôm hazzeh): the exodus created YHWH's šēm (name, reputation) — a demonstration of divine character that endures to Jeremiah's present. The prayer positions the current crisis as requiring an equally definitive act of YHWH-revelation.
21: Wattôṣēʾ ʾet-ʿammĕḵā ʾet-yiśrāʾēl mēʾereṣ miṣrāyim bĕʾōtôt ûḇĕmôpĕtîm ûḇĕyād ḥăzāqâ ûḇiḏrôaʿ nĕṭûyâ ûḇĕmôrāʾ gādôl (bĕyād ḥăzāqâ ûḇiḏrôaʿ nĕṭûyâ): the tricolon of exodus power — mighty hand, outstretched arm, great terror — is the Deuteronomic formula (Deut 4:34; 5:15; 7:19; 11:2; 26:8) that defines YHWH's saving intervention. The prayer's historical rehearsal is not mere recitation but the activation of covenant memory: YHWH who acted thus for Israel in Egypt is the same YHWH being addressed now.
22: Wattittēn lāhem ʾet-hāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt ʾăšer nišbaʿtā lăʾăḇôtām lātēt lāhem ʾereṣ zāḇat ḥālāḇ ûdĕḇāš (ʾereṣ zāḇat ḥālāḇ ûdĕḇāš): the land flowing with milk and honey — the covenant promise to the patriarchs (Exod 3:8, 17; 13:5; 33:3; Lev 20:24; Num 13:27; Deut 6:3 etc.) — was given; Israel entered and took possession. The prayer establishes the full arc of covenant history: promise → exodus → settlement → failure → judgment. This arc creates the context within which the restoration promise of ch31 is the climactic reversal.
23: Wayyāḇōʾû wayyirĕšû ʾōtāh wĕlōʾ šāmĕʿû bĕqôlĕḵā ûḇĕtôrātĕḵā lōʾ-hālāḵû ʾēt kol-ʾăšer-ṣiwwîtā ʾôtām laʿăśôt lōʾ ʿāśû (wĕlōʾ šāmĕʿû bĕqôlĕḵā ûḇĕtôrātĕḵā lōʾ-hālāḵû): the three-fold negation — did not listen to your voice, did not walk in your Torah, did not do all you commanded — summarizes the entire pre-exile history. Wattaḇiʾ ʾălêhem ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ hazzōʾt (Wattaḇiʾ ʾălêhem ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ hazzōʾt): "Therefore you brought all this disaster on them" — the prayer's theology of history is unambiguous: the exile is YHWH's just response to covenant infidelity. Jeremiah does not pray for YHWH to change his assessment; he prays in the context of complete agreement with YHWH's judgment.
24: Hinnēh hasōlĕlôt bāʾû hāʿîr lĕlŏkdāh (Hinnēh hasōlĕlôt bāʾû hāʿîr): the siege ramps (sōlĕlôt) are a visible, physical confirmation of the prophetic word — the disaster YHWH announced has arrived. Wĕhāʿîr nittĕnâ bĕyad hakkaśdîm hammiLlāḥămîm ʿālêhā (Wĕhāʿîr nittĕnâ bĕyad hakkaśdîm): the city is being given (nittĕnâ, Niphal perfect — prophetic perfect of certainty) into the hand of the Chaldeans. ʾĂšer dibbartā hāyâ wĕhinnĕḵā rōʾeh (ʾĂšer dibbartā hāyâ wĕhinnĕḵā rōʾeh): "What you said has come true, and you yourself see it" — the prayer acknowledges the full evidence of judgment before making its final appeal.
25: Wĕʾattâ ʾădōnāy YHWH ʾāmartā ʾēlay qnēh-lĕḵā haśśādeh bakkesep wĕhāʿēd ʿēdîm wĕhāʿîr nittĕnâ bĕyad hakkaśdîm (Wĕʾattâ ʾădōnāy YHWH ʾāmartā ʾēlay qnēh-lĕḵā haśśādeh bakkesep): the prayer's climactic paradox: "You have told me: Buy the field with money and get witnesses — even though the city is being handed to the Chaldeans." This is the prayer of a man caught between two divine words — the word of judgment being enacted outside his window and the word of restoration being enacted through his legal transaction. The prayer does not resolve the paradox; it simply presents it to YHWH, confident that YHWH's answer will be the reconciliation of both words. The faith of vv7-15 was enacted in action; now it is expressed in prayer.
26: Wayhî dĕḇar-YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû lēʾmōr (Wayhî dĕḇar-YHWH ʾel-Yirmĕyāhû lēʾmōr): the divine response comes immediately after the prayer, introduced by the standard prophetic reception formula. The answer occupies the rest of the chapter (vv27-44). The structure — human obedience → prayer → divine word — models prophetic intercession as the movement through which YHWH chooses to explain his redemptive purposes.
27: Hinnēh ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhê kol-bāśār hăyippālēʾ mimmennî kol-dāḇār (ʾănî YHWH ʾĕlōhê kol-bāśār hăyippālēʾ mimmennî kol-dāḇār): YHWH's self-identification: "I am the LORD, the God of all flesh" — kol-bāśār (all flesh) transcends Israel; this is universal sovereignty. The rhetorical question echoes Jeremiah's prayer (v17: lōʾ-yippālēʾ mimmĕḵā kol-dāḇār) and returns it as divine declaration: "Is anything too difficult (yippālēʾ) for me?" The answer to the paradox of v25 is the transcendent power of YHWH over all flesh and all circumstances. Luke 1:37 ("For nothing will be impossible with God") applies the same adynaton formula to the annunciation.
28: Lāḵēn kōh ʾāmar YHWH hinnĕnî nōtēn ʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt bĕyad hakkaśdîm (hinnĕnî nōtēn ʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt bĕyad hakkaśdîm): the divine word confirms the judgment unambiguously — the hinnēnî ("behold me") emphasizes YHWH's personal agency in the handing-over. The restoration promise does not cancel or mitigate the judgment; it follows it. Both words stand: the city will fall (vv28-35) and houses and fields will yet be bought (v15; vv36-44).
29: Ûḇāʾû hakkaśdîm hammiLlāḥămîm ʿal-hāʿîr hazzōʾt wĕhiṣṣîtû ʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt bāʾēš ûśĕrāpûhā (wĕhiṣṣîtû ʾet-hāʿîr hazzōʾt bāʾēš): the burning of the city is stated plainly. Wĕʾet-habbāttîm ʾăšer qiṭṭĕrû ʿal-gaggôtêhem laBBaʿal wĕhissĕkû nĕsāḵîm lēʾlōhîm ʾăḥērîm lĕmaʿan hakʿisēnî (lĕmaʿan hakʿisēnî): "to provoke me to anger" — the rooftop incense and drink offerings to Baal and other gods are explicitly cited as the cause of judgment. The covenant lawsuit (7:31; 19:5 on Molech; 44:17 on the queen of heaven) is the theological basis of the military catastrophe.
30: Kî-hāyû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ûḇĕnê yĕhûdâ raq ʿōśîm hārāʿ bĕʿênay minnĕʿurêhem (raq ʿōśîm hārāʿ bĕʿênay minnĕʿurêhem): "only doing evil in my eyes from their youth" — the indictment is temporal (minnĕʿurêhem, "from their youth") and total (raq ʿōśîm, "doing nothing but evil"). This parallels the earlier accusation against Jerusalem in 2:20-22 ("from of old I broke your yoke") and recalls YHWH's repeated sending of prophets (7:25; 11:7; 25:3-4; 26:5; 29:19; 35:15; 44:4). The long history of repeated sending and repeated rejection is the weight behind the judgment.
31: Kî ʿal-ʾapî wĕʿal-ḥămātî hāytâ lî hāʿîr hazzōʾt lĕminnê hayyôm ʾăšer bānû ʾôtāh wĕʿad hayyôm hazzeh (lĕminnê hayyôm ʾăšer bānû ʾôtāh wĕʿad hayyôm hazzeh): "from the day it was built to this day" — the scope of provocation is the entire history of Jerusalem from its establishment as David's capital. The city's founding did not change the fundamental disposition of its inhabitants. The oracle anticipates the critique in Acts 7:51 (Stephen's speech): the stiff-necked people "always resist the Holy Spirit," from Moses to Jesus.
32: ʿAl kol-rāʿat bĕnê yiśrāʾēl ûḇĕnê yĕhûdâ ʾăšer ʿāśû lĕhaḵʿisēnî hēmmâ malkêhem śārêhem kōhănêhem ûnĕḇiʾêhem wĕʾîš yĕhûdâ wĕyōšĕḇê yĕrûšālāyim: the comprehensive list of offenders — kings, officials, priests, prophets, the men of Judah, and the residents of Jerusalem. No class, no office, no tier of society is exempted. The total coverage is significant for the new covenant promise of ch31:34 ("they will all know me, from the least to the greatest") — the new covenant must be as comprehensive as the indictment.
33: Wayyipnû ʾēlay ʿōrep wĕlōʾ pānîm (Wayyipnû ʾēlay ʿōrep wĕlōʾ pānîm): "they turned their backs (ʿōrep) to me, not their faces (pānîm)" — the spatial metaphor for apostasy: to turn the neck/back is to reject relationship; to turn the face is to enter it. Ezekiel uses the same imagery (Ezek 8:16: worshipers "turning their backs to the temple of the LORD and facing east"). Wĕlammēd ʾôtām haškēm wĕlammēd wĕʾênām šōmĕʿîm lāqaḥat mûsār (haškēm wĕlammēd): "rising early to teach" — the haškēm (rising early) formula used throughout Jeremiah for urgent, persistent prophetic address (7:13; 7:25; 11:7; 25:3-4) is now applied to divine teaching: YHWH himself persistently taught, and they persistently refused correction (mûsār).
34: Wayyāśîmû šiqqûṣêhem babbayit ʾăšer niqrāʾ-šĕmî ʿālāyw lĕṭammĕʾô (šiqqûṣêhem babbayit ʾăšer niqrāʾ-šĕmî ʿālāyw): detestable things (šiqqûṣîm — the technical term for idols, Deut 7:26; 29:17; Ezek 5:11) in the temple that bears YHWH's name. The profanation of the name (niqrāʾ-šĕmî ʿālāyw) is the precise violation of the Decalogue's third commandment (Exod 20:7: "you shall not take the name of the LORD your God in vain") applied institutionally. The temple, intended to be the house of YHWH's name, became the house of his rivals.
35: Wayyiḇnû ʾet-bāmôt habbaʿal ʾăšer bĕgêʾ ḇen-Hinnōm lĕhaʿăḇîr ʾet-bĕnêhem wĕʾet-bĕnôtêhem lammōleḵ ʾăšer lōʾ-ṣiwwîtîm wĕlōʾ ʿālĕtâ ʿal-libbî: the triple negation for Molech child sacrifice — lōʾ-ṣiwwîtîm (I did not command them), wĕlōʾ ʿālĕtâ ʿal-libbî (it did not come up on my heart/mind) — matches 7:31 and 19:5 exactly. The repetition in three separate contexts (7, 19, 32) functions as legal triple attestation: YHWH's total disavowal of the practice is absolute and documented. The phrase lōʾ ʿālĕtâ ʿal-libbî (it never entered my mind) is an extreme statement — the very thought was alien to YHWH's nature.
36: Wĕʿattâ lāḵēn kōh-ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-hāʿîr hazzōʾt ʾăšer ʾattem ʾōmĕrîm nittĕnâ bĕyad melek bāḇel bĕḥereḇ ûḇārāʿāḇ ûḇaddāḇer (Wĕʿattâ lāḵēn): the transitional wĕʿattâ lāḵēn ("now therefore") pivots from indictment (vv28-35) to restoration promise (vv36-44). The city being delivered by sword, famine, and plague is the acknowledged reality; what follows is not a denial of it but YHWH's word about what comes after.
37: Hinnĕnî mĕqabbĕṣām mikkol-hāʾărāṣôt ʾăšer hiddaḥtîm šām bĕʾappî ûḇăḥămātî ûḇĕqeṣep gādôl (mikkol-hāʾărāṣôt ʾăšer hiddaḥtîm šām): the gathering from all the lands is YHWH's direct reversal of the scattering. bĕʾappî ûḇăḥămātî ûḇĕqeṣep gādôl (in my anger, fury, and great wrath) — the same three terms used in ch30's opening oracle — acknowledges that the scattering was genuine divine anger, not merely Babylonian military superiority. Wahăšîḇōtîm ʾel-hammāqôm hazzeh wĕhôšaḇtîm lāḇeṭaḥ (wĕhôšaḇtîm lāḇeṭaḥ): the security (beṭaḥ) of the restored community is YHWH's guarantee, not their own military capacity.
38: Wĕhāyû-lî lĕʿām wăʾănî ʾehyeh lāhem lēʾlōhîm (Wĕhāyû-lî lĕʿām wăʾănî ʾehyeh lāhem lēʾlōhîm): the covenant formula in its restored form. The formula appears in both the Sinai covenant (Lev 26:12; Exod 6:7) and the new covenant (Jer 31:33; Ezek 36:28; 37:27; Rev 21:3). The simplicity of the declaration — "they will be my people, and I will be their God" — contains all covenant theology. In 32:38 it is restoration of what was broken; in 31:33 it is the new covenant's fulfillment; in Rev 21:3 it is the eschatological completion of the entire biblical trajectory.
39: Wĕnātattî lāhem lēḇ ʾeḥād wĕdereḵ ʾeḥād lĕyirʾâ ʾôtî kol-hayyāmîm lĕṭôḇ lāhem wĕliḇnêhem ʾaḥărêhem (lēḇ ʾeḥād wĕdereḵ ʾeḥād): "one heart and one way" — the lēḇ ʾeḥād (one heart) is the community-level version of the new covenant's internal Torah (31:33). Ezekiel 11:19-20 and 36:26 give the mechanism: removal of the heart of stone, implantation of a heart of flesh with the Spirit. lĕyirʾâ ʾôtî kol-hayyāmîm (to fear me all the days) — the yirʾat YHWH (fear of YHWH) is not an emotion to be cultivated but a divine gift to be given: YHWH will put his fear in their hearts (v40).
40: Wĕkāratî lāhem bĕrît ʿôlām ʾăšer lōʾ-ʾāšûḇ mēʾaḥărêhem lĕhêṭîḇ ʾôtām wĕʾet-yirʾātî ʾettēn bĕlibbām lĕḇiltî sûr mēʿālay: the everlasting covenant (bĕrît ʿôlām) contains the resolution of the covenant problem — YHWH guarantees his own fidelity (lōʾ-ʾāšûḇ mēʾaḥărêhem, "I will never turn away from doing good to them") and the people's fidelity (wĕʾet-yirʾātî ʾettēn bĕlibbām lĕḇiltî sûr mēʿālay, "I will put my fear in their hearts so they will not turn away from me"). The failure of the Sinai covenant was Israel's unfaithfulness; the new everlasting covenant removes the structural cause of that failure by making human faithfulness a divine provision, not a human achievement. Hebrews 13:20-21 cites this "eternal covenant" as the ground of the divine work in believers.
41: Wĕśaśtî ʿălêhem lĕhêṭîḇ ʾôtām wĕnāṭaʿtîm bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt bĕʾemet bĕḵol-libbî ûḇĕḵol-napšî (Wĕśaśtî ʿălêhem lĕhêṭîḇ ʾôtām): "I will rejoice in doing good to them" — śûś (to rejoice, exult) is divine delight in the act of blessing. bĕḵol-libbî ûḇĕḵol-napšî (bĕḵol-libbî ûḇĕḵol-napšî): "with all my heart and all my soul" — YHWH applies the Shema language to himself (Deut 6:5: "love YHWH your God with all your heart and all your soul"). The command Israel failed to obey, YHWH fulfills toward them: his covenant commitment is a whole-hearted, whole-being investment in their good. Zephaniah 3:17 echoes this: "He will rejoice over you with gladness; he will quiet you by his love; he will exult over you with loud singing."
42: Kî ḵōh ʾāmar YHWH kaʾăšer hēḇēʾtî ʾel-hāʿām hazzeh ʾēt kol-hārāʿâ haggĕdōlâ hazzōʾt kēn ʾānōḵî mēḇîʾ ʾălêhem ʾet kol-haṭṭôḇâ ʾăšer ʾānōḵî dōḇēr ʾălêhem (kaʾăšer hēḇēʾtî... kēn ʾānōḵî mēḇîʾ): "Just as I have brought all this great disaster... so I will bring on them all the good." The kāʾăšer... kēn (as... so) structure uses the certainty of the judgment-fulfillment as the guarantee of the restoration-fulfillment. If the word of judgment was reliable (and v24 confirms it is being enacted), the word of restoration is equally reliable. The same sovereignty that brought the disaster brings the good.
43: Wĕniknû haśśādôt bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt ʾăšer ʾattem ʾōmĕrîm šĕmāmâ hî mēʾên ʾādām ûḇĕhēmâ nittĕnâ bĕyad hakkaśdîm (ʾăšer ʾattem ʾōmĕrîm šĕmāmâ hî mēʾên ʾādām ûḇĕhēmâ): "the land about which you say it is desolate, without man or beast." The human perception of total desolation (siege, fall, deportation) is acknowledged as the present reality. Wĕniknû haśśādôt bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt (Wĕniknû haśśādôt bāʾāreṣ hazzōʾt): "Fields will be bought in this land" — the prophecy of vv9-15 is now declared as YHWH's own word: the field purchase is the enacted sign of what YHWH has determined to do.
44: Śādôt bakkesep yiqnû wĕkātôḇ bassēper wĕḥātôm wĕhāʿēd ʿēdîm bĕʾereṣ Binyāmîn ûḇisĕḇîḇôt yĕrûšālāyim ûḇĕʿārê yĕhûdâ ûḇĕʿārê hāhār ûḇĕʿārê haššĕpēlâ ûḇĕʿārê hannegeḇ kî ʾāšîḇ ʾet-šĕḇûtām nĕʾum-YHWH: the final verse encompasses the full geographical range of the land of Israel — Benjamin (Jeremiah's own tribe, where Anathoth is), the environs of Jerusalem, the towns of Judah, the hill country (hāhār), the Shephelah (haššĕpēlâ), and the Negev. The comprehensive geography is the scope of the promise: the entire land will be restored. The chapter closes with the covenantal formula kî ʾāšîḇ ʾet-šĕḇûtām ("for I will restore their fortunes") — a technical term (šûḇ šĕḇût/šĕḇît) used repeatedly in restoration oracles (30:3, 18; 31:23; 33:7, 11, 26; Job 42:10; Ezek 16:53; Amos 9:14; Zeph 3:20) for the comprehensive reversal of exile. The field purchase of vv7-15 is thus the sign of what v44 declares as YHWH's absolute promise.
